>    - **Expected pattern**: Dose-response relationship (Normal < Overweight < Obese < Severely Obese)
>    - **Biological basis**:
>      - **Insulin resistance**: Adipose tissue releases inflammatory cytokines, free fatty acids
>      - **β-cell dysfunction**: Chronic hyperinsulinemia → pancreatic exhaustion
>      - **Ectopic fat**: Visceral and hepatic fat accumulation impairs glucose metabolism
>      - **Adipokine dysregulation**: Reduced adiponectin, elevated leptin, resistin
>    - **Anticipated effect size**: Small-to-medium ($\eta^2$ ~ 0.03-0.10)
>      - BMI is important but not sole determinant of glycemic control
>      - Genetic factors, physical activity, diet quality also critical
>      - Among non-diabetics, effect may be smaller (restricted range of HbA1c)
>    - **Expected HbA1c progression** (approximate means):
>      - Normal weight (BMI <25): ~5.0-5.2%
>      - Overweight (BMI 25-29.9): ~5.2-5.4%
>      - Obese (BMI 30-39.9): ~5.4-5.8%
>      - Severely obese (BMI ≥40): ~5.8-6.2%
>    - **Post-hoc expectations**:
>      - Normal vs. Overweight: May not differ significantly (both within normal glycemic range)
>      - Normal vs. Obese: Should show significant difference (~0.4-0.6% HbA1c increase)
>      - Obese vs. Severely Obese: Significant if sample includes diabetics; borderline if only non-diabetics
>      - **Critical comparison**: Normal weight vs. severely obese should show largest difference
>    - **Clinical implications**:
>      - If significant with medium effect, confirms BMI as modifiable risk factor for dysglycemia
>      - Supports weight loss interventions for diabetes prevention
>      - Quantifies glycemic benefit of achieving lower BMI categories
>      - If NOT significant, suggests other factors (e.g., diet quality, genetics) dominate over BMI alone
>    - **Subgroup considerations**:
>      - Effect likely stronger among those with prediabetes or diabetes
>      - May be attenuated in young, metabolically healthy obese individuals
>
> **6. ANOVA Assumptions and Robustness**:
>
>    **A. Independence of Observations**
>    - **Requirement**: Each observation must be independent (no repeated measures, no clustering)
>    - **NHANES consideration**: Complex survey design includes household clustering
>      - Violation: Family members within households may be correlated
>      - Impact: Standard errors underestimated → inflated Type I error (false positives)
>      - **Mitigation**: Use survey-weighted ANOVA if weights available, or acknowledge as limitation
>
>    **B. Normality of Residuals**
>    - **Requirement**: Within each group, the outcome should follow a normal distribution (or residuals should be normally distributed)
>    - **Shapiro-Wilk results**: Earlier tests showed departures from normality for BMI, SBP, HbA1c
>    - **Robustness**: ANOVA is remarkably robust to non-normality when:
>      - Sample sizes are large (n > 30 per group, ideally > 100)
>      - Group sizes are roughly balanced
>      - Violations are moderate (slight skewness acceptable; extreme outliers problematic)
>    - **Central Limit Theorem**: With n>1,000 total, sampling distribution of means approaches normality even if raw data are non-normal
>    - **Result**: Non-normality is **not a major concern** for this analysis given large sample
>    - **Alternative if needed**: Kruskal-Wallis test (non-parametric equivalent) if extreme outliers or severe skewness
>
>    **C. Homogeneity of Variance (Homoscedasticity)**
>    - **Requirement**: Within-group variances should be approximately equal across all groups
>    - **Test**: Levene's test or Bartlett's test (conducted separately in variance test section)
>    - **Violation consequences**: If variances unequal and group sizes unequal → Type I error inflation
>    - **Robustness**: ANOVA fairly robust if:
>      - Largest variance < 4× smallest variance (rule of thumb)
>      - Group sizes are similar (balanced design)
>    - **Alternatives if violated**:
>      - Welch's ANOVA: Does not assume equal variances (analogous to Welch's t-test)
>      - Data transformation: Log-transform or square-root transform to stabilize variance
>      - Non-parametric test: Kruskal-Wallis does not assume equal variances
>
> **7. Clinical vs. Statistical Significance in ANOVA**:
>    - **Scenario analysis**:
>
>      | Test       | p      | Eta_Sq | interpretation  | Action           |
>      |------------|--------|--------|-----------------|------------------|
>      | SBP by Age | <0.001 |   0.15 |    Sig, large   | Age is major BP determinant |
>      | BMI by Age |  0.003 |   0.02 |    Sig, small   | Age explains little BMI variance |
>      | A1C by BMI |  0.08  |   0.08 |   Non-sig, med  |Underpowered; need larger n |
>      | A1C by BMI | <0.001 |  0.005 | Sig, negligible | Not clinically useful |
>
>    - **Interpretation principle**: Prioritize tests with **both significance AND meaningful effect size** $(\eta^2 \ge 0.06)$
>
> **8. Integration with Regression Modeling**:
>    - **ANOVA as regression**: One-way ANOVA is mathematically equivalent to linear regression with categorical predictor
>      - ANOVA F-test = Overall F-test for regression model
>      - $\eta^2$ = R² (proportion of variance explained)
>      - Post-hoc comparisons = testing individual dummy variable coefficients
>    - **Implications for multivariable models**:
>      - If age shows large $\eta^2$ for SBP ($\eta^2$>0.14), age will be significant covariate in BP regression models
>      - If BMI categories show medium $\eta^2$ for HbA1c ($\eta^2$=0.08), expect BMI to remain significant predictor after adjusting for other variables
>      - Effect sizes provide **upper bound** for multivariable R²; additional predictors will increase R² but with diminishing returns
>    - **Interaction considerations**:
>      - Significant ANOVAs suggest testing interactions in regression (e.g., age × gender for BP)
>      - Post-hoc patterns inform interaction hypotheses (e.g., if elderly show disproportionate HbA1c increase, test age × BMI interaction)
>
> **9. Comparison with T-Tests**:
>    - **When groups = 2**: ANOVA F-test is equivalent to two-sample t-test (F = t²)
>      - Example: If comparing males vs. females only, ANOVA and t-test yield identical p-values
>    - **Advantage of ANOVA**: Tests multiple groups simultaneously while controlling Type I error
>    - **Advantage of t-test**: Provides Cohen's d (standardized mean difference); ANOVA provides $\eta^2$ (variance explained)
>    - **Complementary information**:
>      - Cohen's d quantifies **pairwise effect magnitude** (difference in standard deviation units)
>      - $\eta^2$ quantifies **overall group effect** (percentage of variance explained)
>
> **10. Post-Hoc Interpretation Guidelines**:
>    - **Reading Tukey HSD output**:
>      - Example: "30-44 vs 18-29: difference = 2.5, CI [1.2, 3.8], p = 0.0002"
>        - Interpretation: "Adults aged 30-44 have BMI 2.5 kg/m² higher than 18-29 year-olds (95% CI [1.2, 3.8]). This difference is statistically significant (p = 0.0002) after adjusting for multiple comparisons."
>    - **Pattern recognition**:
>      - **Monotonic increase**: Each group significantly higher than previous (e.g., SBP by age)
>      - **Threshold effect**: Only extreme groups differ from reference (e.g., only severely obese differ from normal weight)
>      - **U-shaped**: Youngest and oldest differ from middle (e.g., BMI by age if observed)
>    - **Clinical translation**:
>      - Identify "clinically important differences": raw difference ≥ minimal clinically important difference (MCID)
>        - SBP: MCID ~5 mmHg (cardiovascular risk difference)
>        - BMI: MCID ~2-3 kg/m² (weight category change)
>        - HbA1c: MCID ~0.5% (diabetes diagnosis/control threshold)
>      - A difference can be statistically significant but clinically trivial (e.g., 0.1% HbA1c difference)
>
> **11. Reporting Best Practices**:
>    - **Complete ANOVA reporting**:
>      - "One-way ANOVA revealed significant differences in systolic blood pressure across age groups (F(3,996) = 45.2, p < 0.001, $\eta^2$ = 0.12). Age group explained 12% of SBP variance, representing a medium-to-large effect. Tukey HSD post-hoc tests indicated that each successive age group had significantly higher SBP than the previous (all pairwise p < 0.01), with the largest difference between youngest (18-29: M = 110 mmHg) and oldest (65+: M = 135 mmHg) groups (mean difference = 25 mmHg, 95% CI [20, 30])."
>    - **Essential elements**: F-statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value, effect size ($\eta^2$), post-hoc results with specific comparisons, means for context
>    - **Visual presentation**: Include boxplots or mean plots with error bars to show group differences visually
>
> **12. Limitations Specific to These ANOVAs**:
>    - **Age categorization**: Grouping continuous age into categories loses information
>      - Alternative: Linear regression with continuous age may be more powerful
>      - Advantage of categories: Easier to detect non-linear patterns (e.g., quadratic relationships)
>    - **Cross-sectional design**: Cannot distinguish age effects from cohort effects
>      - Observed "age differences" may reflect generational differences (older cohort exposed to different lifestyle/environment)
>      - Longitudinal data needed to confirm aging effects
>    - **Unequal group sizes**: If some age/BMI groups are much smaller, power to detect differences in those groups is reduced
>    - **Multiple comparisons within post-hoc**: Even with Tukey adjustment, 10+ pairwise comparisons increase false positive risk

\newpage

## Variance Test

```{r variance-tests}
## Variance Tests
variance_results <- list()

recode_diabetes_2grp <- function(df) {
  df %>%
    filter(diabetes %in% c("No", "Prediabetes", "Yes")) %>%
    mutate(
      diabetes_2grp = case_when(
        diabetes == "No" ~ "No",
        diabetes %in% c("Prediabetes", "Yes") ~ "Pre/Yes"
      )
    )
}

# 1. SBP Variance by Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)
if (all(c("sbp", "diabetes") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  sbp_subset <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    recode_diabetes_2grp() %>%
    drop_na(sbp, diabetes_2grp)

  sbp_var <- var.test(sbp ~ diabetes_2grp, data = sbp_subset)

  variance_results[["SBP Variance by Diabetes Status"]] <- data.frame(
    Outcome        = "SBP",
    Grouping       = "Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)",
    Test           = "F-test",
    Variance_Ratio = round(sbp_var$estimate, 3),
    F_Statistic    = round(sbp_var$statistic, 3),
    DF1            = sbp_var$parameter[1],
    DF2            = sbp_var$parameter[2],
    P_Value        = format.pval(sbp_var$p.value, digits = 3),
    Significant    = ifelse(sbp_var$p.value < 0.05, "Yes", "No")
  )
}

# 2. A1C Variance by Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)
if (all(c("a1c", "diabetes") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  a1c_subset <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    recode_diabetes_2grp() %>%
    drop_na(a1c, diabetes_2grp)

  a1c_var <- var.test(a1c ~ diabetes_2grp, data = a1c_subset)

  variance_results[["A1C Variance by Diabetes Status"]] <- data.frame(
    Outcome        = "A1C",
    Grouping       = "Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)",
    Test           = "F-test",
    Variance_Ratio = round(a1c_var$estimate, 3),
    F_Statistic    = round(a1c_var$statistic, 3),
    DF1            = a1c_var$parameter[1],
    DF2            = a1c_var$parameter[2],
    P_Value        = format.pval(a1c_var$p.value, digits = 3),
    Significant    = ifelse(a1c_var$p.value < 0.05, "Yes", "No")
  )
}

# 3. BMI Variance by Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)
if (all(c("bmi", "diabetes") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  bmi_subset <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    recode_diabetes_2grp() %>%
    drop_na(bmi, diabetes_2grp)

  bmi_var <- var.test(bmi ~ diabetes_2grp, data = bmi_subset)

  variance_results[["BMI Variance by Diabetes Status"]] <- data.frame(
    Outcome        = "BMI",
    Grouping       = "Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)",
    Test           = "F-test",
    Variance_Ratio = round(bmi_var$estimate, 3),
    F_Statistic    = round(bmi_var$statistic, 3),
    DF1            = bmi_var$parameter[1],
    DF2            = bmi_var$parameter[2],
    P_Value        = format.pval(bmi_var$p.value, digits = 3),
    Significant    = ifelse(bmi_var$p.value < 0.05, "Yes", "No")
  )
}

# 4. Income-Poverty Ratio Variance by Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)
if (all(c("income_poverty_ratio", "diabetes") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  ip_subset <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    recode_diabetes_2grp() %>%
    drop_na(income_poverty_ratio, diabetes_2grp)

  ip_var <- var.test(income_poverty_ratio ~ diabetes_2grp, data = ip_subset)

  variance_results[["Income-Poverty Ratio Variance by Diabetes"]] <- data.frame(
    Outcome        = "Income-Poverty Ratio",
    Grouping       = "Diabetes (Pre/Yes vs No)",
    Test           = "F-test",
    Variance_Ratio = round(ip_var$estimate, 3),
    F_Statistic    = round(ip_var$statistic, 3),
    DF1            = ip_var$parameter[1],
    DF2            = ip_var$parameter[2],
    P_Value        = format.pval(ip_var$p.value, digits = 3),
    Significant    = ifelse(ip_var$p.value < 0.05, "Yes", "No")
  )
}

## Combine & Display Variance Test Results 
if (length(variance_results) > 0) {

    variance_df <- do.call(rbind, variance_results)
    rownames(variance_df) <- NULL

    variance_df <- variance_df %>% dplyr::select(-Test)
    
    kable(
        variance_df,
        caption = "Variance Tests for Group Comparisons (p < 0.05 indicates significant difference)",
        booktabs = TRUE,
        format = "latex",
        longtable =TRUE
    ) %>%
      kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"), 
                    font_size = 9, full_width = FALSE)
}
```

**Interpretation:**

1. SBP Variance by Diabetes Status — Significant Difference (p = 4.5e−05): Combining Prediabetes and Diabetes into one group reveals a significant variance difference in SBP compared with individuals without diabetes. This suggests that once early-stage dysglycemia (Prediabetes) is included, the overall diabetic spectrum shows more heterogeneous blood-pressure variability. This aligns with clinical understanding that impaired metabolic regulation interacts with cardiovascular function, producing wider variability in SBP even before full diabetes diagnosis.

2. A1C Variance by Diabetes Status — Significant Difference (p < 2e−16): This finding is highly consistent with medical knowledge: diabetes patients differ widely in disease duration, treatment adherence, and glucose regulation, all of which lead to greater heterogeneity in A1C. This variance difference highlights the diverse glycemic control patterns within the diabetic population.

3. BMI Variance by Diabetes Status — Significant Difference (p = 0.000453): This reflects the well-documented heterogeneity of diabetes: while many patients develop diabetes through obesity-related pathways, others develop it despite having normal or only moderately elevated BMI. The result supports the notion that diabetes is not tied to a single body-weight profile. Not all diabetic individuals are obese, and not all individuals with high BMI develop diabetes, making BMI variance naturally diverse.

4. Income-Poverty Ratio Variance by Diabetes Status — No Significant Difference (p = 0.822): While diabetes is associated with socioeconomic disadvantage at the mean level, its variance does not differ significantly between groups. Both diabetic and non-diabetic populations contain individuals across diverse SES strata. This suggests that SES influences diabetes risk mainly through level differences, e.g., higher prevalence in low-SES groups, rather than through variability differences.

Overall, these results demonstrate that merging Prediabetes and Diabetes into a single dysglycemic group reveals substantially greater variability in key physiological indicators—specifically SBP, A1C, and BMI—relative to individuals without diabetes, while socioeconomic variability remains comparable across groups. This pattern underscores diabetes as a heterogeneous clinical condition arising from multiple biological and behavioral pathways, which generate wide dispersion in metabolic and cardiovascular measures even within the same diagnostic category. By identifying which outcomes differ not only in mean levels but also in variance, this analysis complements earlier comparisons and clarifies the dimensions along which diabetic heterogeneity manifests. 

\newpage

# Statistical Modeling

```{r define-predictors, include=FALSE}
# Define predictor set for modeling
predictor_vars <- c("age", "gender", "bmi", "sbp", "physical_activity_freq", "income_poverty_ratio")
```

## Correlation Inference
```{r correlation-inference, fig.height=6, fig.width=8}
library(corrplot)
# Select numeric variables for correlation
cor_vars <- nhanes_analysis[, c("age", "bmi", "sbp", "dbp", "a1c", "glucose", "sleep_hours", "physical_activity_freq")]

# Spearman correlation (use Spearman as data is non-normal)
cor_matrix <- cor(cor_vars, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")

# Display correlation matrix
print(cor_matrix)

# Correlation heatmap
corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper",
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.7,
         tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45,
         title = "Correlation Matrix of Health Variables (Spearman)",
         mar = c(0,0,2,0))

# Example: Test correlation between age and BMI
cor.test(nhanes_analysis$age, nhanes_analysis$bmi, method = "spearman")

# Loop through all pairs
pairs_combo <- combn(names(cor_vars), 2)
results <- apply(pairs_combo, 2, function(x) {
  test <- cor.test(cor_vars[[x[1]]], cor_vars[[x[2]]], method = "spearman")
  data.frame(var1 = x[1], var2 = x[2], estimate = test$estimate,
             p.value = test$p.value)
})
cor_results <- do.call(rbind, results)
print(cor_results)

# Example: Age vs BMI
ggplot(nhanes_analysis, aes(x = age, y = bmi)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "BMI vs Age", x = "Age (years)", y = "BMI") +
  theme_minimal()

```

> **Interpretation:**
> The correlation analysis examines bivariate relationships among continuous health variables using Spearman's rank correlation coefficient:
>
> **1. Correlation Coefficient Choice: Spearman vs. Pearson**:
>    - **Spearman's $\rho$ (rho)**: Measures **monotonic relationships** based on rank ordering, not raw values
>      - Formula: $\rho$ = 1 - $\frac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}$ where $d_i$ = difference in ranks
>      - **Advantages**: Robust to outliers, does not assume normality or linearity, handles ordinal data
>      - **Interpretation**: $\rho$ = 1 (perfect positive monotonic), $\rho$ = 0 (no monotonic relationship), $\rho$ = -1 (perfect negative monotonic)
>    - **Pearson's r**: Measures **linear relationships** based on actual values
>      - Assumes bivariate normality, sensitive to outliers, only captures linear associations
>    - **Why Spearman chosen**: Shapiro-Wilk tests confirmed non-normal distributions for all continuous variables
>      - Spearman provides valid inference without normality assumption
>      - Can detect non-linear monotonic relationships (e.g., exponential, logarithmic) that Pearson would miss
>    - **Trade-off**: Spearman has slightly less statistical power than Pearson when data are truly normal and linear
>
> **2. Correlation Strength Interpretation Guidelines**:
>    - **Magnitude** (Cohen, 1988; absolute value):
>      - |$\rho$| < 0.10: Negligible/trivial correlation
>      - |$\rho$| = 0.10-0.30: Small/weak correlation
>      - |$\rho$| = 0.30-0.50: Medium/moderate correlation
>      - |$\rho$| > 0.50: Large/strong correlation
>    - **Statistical significance** (p-value): Whether correlation differs from zero
>      - p < 0.05: Evidence that correlation exists in population
>      - With large samples (n>1,000), even tiny correlations (|$\rho$|<0.10) become statistically significant
>    - **Practical significance**: Focus on magnitude, not just p-value
>      - $\rho$ = 0.08 with p<0.001: Statistically significant but practically negligible (explains <1% variance)
>      - $\rho$ = 0.45 with p=0.06: Not statistically significant but potentially meaningful (may indicate insufficient power)
>    - **Variance explained**: $R^2 = \rho^2$ approximates proportion of variance shared
>      - $\rho$ = 0.20 → R² $\approx$ 0.04 (4% shared variance)
>      - $\rho$ = 0.45 → R² $\approx$ 0.20 (20% shared variance)
>      - $\rho$ = 0.70 → R² $\approx$ 0.49 (49% shared variance)
>
> **3. Statistical Significance Testing**:
>    - **Null hypothesis** ($H_0$): $\rho$ = 0 (no monotonic relationship in population)
>    - **Test statistic**: Approximated by t-distribution with df = n-2
>    - **p-value interpretation**: Probability of observing correlation this strong if true population correlation were zero
>    - **Multiple comparisons issue**: With 8 variables, there are $\binom{8}{2} = 28$ pairwise correlations
>      - Probability of ≥1 false positive at α=0.05: $1-(1-0.05)^{28} = 0.76$ (76%!)
>      - **Recommendation**: Apply Bonferroni correction (α' = 0.05/28 = 0.0018) for conservative inference
>      - Correlations with p < 0.002 are most robust; 0.002 < p < 0.05 should be interpreted cautiously
>
> **4. Detailed Findings by Variable Pair**:
>
>    **A. Age Correlations - Strong Effects of Aging**
>
>    - **Age × HbA1c**: $\rho$ = 0.455, p < 0.001 (large positive correlation)
>      - **Interpretation**: Strong monotonic increase in HbA1c with age; older adults have substantially higher long-term glucose
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.21 (21% of HbA1c variance associated with age)
>      - **Biological mechanisms**:
>        - **β-cell senescence**: Pancreatic insulin secretion declines with age
>        - **Insulin resistance**: Tissue sensitivity to insulin decreases due to cellular aging, inflammation
>        - **Body composition changes**: Increased visceral fat, decreased muscle mass → impaired glucose disposal
>        - **Medication use**: Older adults more likely on diabetogenic medications (steroids, thiazides)
>      - **Clinical implications**: Age is the strongest single predictor of HbA1c in this correlation matrix
>      - **Confounding concern**: Age-HbA1c relationship may confound other associations (e.g., BMI-HbA1c)
>      - **Follow-up**: Age-stratified analyses or age-adjusted partial correlations may reveal different patterns by age group
>
>    - **Age × SBP**: $\rho$ = 0.400, p < 0.001 (large positive correlation)
>      - **Interpretation**: Strong monotonic increase in systolic blood pressure with age
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.16 (16% of SBP variance associated with age)
>      - **Biological mechanisms**: Arterial stiffening, endothelial dysfunction, reduced nitric oxide, baroreceptor blunting
>      - **Clinical context**: Expected ~20-30 mmHg increase from age 20 to 80
>      - **Comparison with ANOVA**: This correlation complements the ANOVA (SBP by age group) with continuous relationship
>      - **Implication**: Age must be controlled in any multivariable model predicting SBP or HbA1c
>
>    - **Age × DBP**: Likely weaker than Age × SBP (typically $\rho$ ~ 0.15-0.25)
>      - DBP often peaks in middle age then plateaus or declines (arterial stiffness affects systolic > diastolic)
>      - If correlation is weak/non-significant, confirms isolated systolic hypertension pattern in elderly
>
>    - **Age × BMI**: Expected weak correlation ($\rho$ ~ 0.05-0.15) due to inverted U-shape observed in ANOVA
>      - Linear correlation (Spearman) may not capture non-monotonic relationship well
>      - Scatterplot (age vs. BMI) should show curved pattern with peak in middle age
>
> **B. BMI Correlations - Metabolic Syndrome Linkages**
>
>    - **BMI × HbA1c**: $\rho$ = 0.202, p < 0.001 (small-to-medium positive correlation)
>      - **Interpretation**: Modest monotonic increase in HbA1c with higher BMI
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.04 (4% of HbA1c variance associated with BMI)
>      - **Biological basis**: Obesity → insulin resistance → β-cell dysfunction → hyperglycemia
>      - **Clinical context**: While statistically significant, BMI alone explains relatively little HbA1c variance
>        - Other factors dominate: genetics, diet quality, physical activity, medications
>      - **Why correlation is modest**:
>        - Not all obese individuals are hyperglycemic (metabolically healthy obesity exists)
>        - Not all diabetics are obese (lean diabetes, especially in certain ethnicities)
>        - Relationship may be non-linear (threshold effect at BMI ≥30)
>      - **Comparison with ANOVA**: ANOVA (HbA1c by BMI category) may show stronger effect if relationship is non-linear
>
>    - **BMI × Glucose**: $\rho$ = 0.237, p < 0.001 (small-to-medium positive correlation)
>      - **Interpretation**: Similar to BMI-HbA1c but slightly stronger with fasting glucose
>      - **Why stronger than HbA1c**: Glucose reflects acute metabolic state more sensitively
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.06 (6% shared variance)
>
>    - **BMI × DBP**: $\rho$ = 0.253, p < 0.001 (small-to-medium positive correlation)
>      - **Interpretation**: Moderate positive association between obesity and diastolic pressure
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.06 (6% of DBP variance associated with BMI)
>      - **Biological mechanisms**:
>        - Increased blood volume and cardiac output in obesity
>        - Sympathetic nervous system overactivity
>        - Sodium retention and vascular remodeling
>        - Adipokine-mediated vasoconstriction
>      - **Clinical note**: Obesity affects diastolic > systolic in younger adults; pattern reverses with age
>
>    - **BMI × SBP**: Expected similar or slightly weaker than BMI × DBP ($\rho$ ~ 0.20-0.30)
>      - Check if BMI-SBP correlation is weaker than age-SBP correlation (age dominates systolic BP)
>
> **C. Sleep Correlations - Emerging Risk Factor**
>
>    - **Sleep Hours × HbA1c**: $\rho$ = -0.092, p = 0.033 (small negative correlation, borderline significance)
>      - **Interpretation**: Weak inverse relationship—fewer sleep hours associated with slightly higher HbA1c
>      - **Variance explained**: R² $\approx$ 0.008 (0.8% shared variance—negligible)
>      - **Statistical concerns**:
>        - p = 0.033 > Bonferroni-corrected threshold (p = 0.0018)
>        - Does **not** survive multiple comparisons correction
>        - May be a **false positive** among 28 tests
>        - Should be interpreted cautiously and require replication
>      - **Biological plausibility**:
>        - Sleep deprivation → cortisol elevation → insulin resistance
>        - Circadian disruption → impaired glucose tolerance
>        - Sleep loss → increased appetite hormones (ghrelin) → weight gain → dysglycemia
>      - **Clinical significance**: Even if real, effect is too small to be clinically useful (explains <1% variance)
>      - **Measurement issues**:
>        - Self-reported sleep hours may be inaccurate
>        - Does not capture sleep quality, apnea, or circadian misalignment
>        - U-shaped relationship possible (very short AND very long sleep both harmful)
>      - **Recommendation**: Include sleep in multivariable models but don't overinterpret this bivariate correlation
>
>    - **Sleep Hours × Glucose**: $\rho$ = -0.074, p = 0.045 (small negative correlation, not significant after correction)
>      - **Interpretation**: Even weaker than sleep-HbA1c relationship
>      - **Significance**: p = 0.045 does not survive Bonferroni correction (p < 0.0018)
>      - Likely a false positive or trivially small true effect
>      - Do not draw clinical conclusions from this correlation alone
>
> **D. Blood Pressure Correlations**
>
>    - **SBP × DBP**: Expected strong positive correlation ($\rho$ ~ 0.50-0.70)
>      - Both components of blood pressure; physiologically coupled
>      - High correlation expected but not perfect (isolated systolic/diastolic hypertension exists)
>
>    - **SBP × Glucose/HbA1c**: Likely weak-to-moderate ($\rho$ ~ 0.15-0.25)
>      - Reflects shared cardiometabolic risk factors (obesity, inflammation, insulin resistance)
>      - Weaker than age correlations because relationship is indirect
>
> **E. Physical Activity Correlations**
>
>    - Expected **negative correlations** with BMI, HbA1c, glucose, BP (protective effect)
>    - Likely **weak correlations** ($\rho$ ~ -0.10 to -0.20) due to:
>      - Self-report bias (overestimation of activity)
>      - Wide individual variation in metabolic response to exercise
>      - Reverse causation (sick individuals exercise less)
>    - If correlations are non-significant, suggests physical activity measurement may be inadequate
>
> **5. Correlation Matrix Visualization Interpretation**:
>    - **Heatmap color intensity**: Darker colors = stronger correlations
>    - **Clustering patterns**: Look for blocks of high correlations indicating related variable groups
>      - Expected cluster 1: Age, HbA1c, glucose (metabolic aging)
>      - Expected cluster 2: SBP, DBP, age (cardiovascular aging)
>      - Expected cluster 3: BMI, DBP, HbA1c (metabolic syndrome components)
>    - **Symmetric matrix**: Upper triangle mirrors lower triangle ($\rho$(X,Y) = $\rho$(Y,X))
>    - **Diagonal = 1.0**: Perfect correlation of each variable with itself
>
> **6. Causality and Interpretation Caveats**:
>    - **Correlation ≠ Causation**: Spearman's $\rho$ measures association, not causal direction
>      - **Example**: Age × HbA1c correlation ($\rho$ = 0.455) does NOT prove aging causes high glucose
>      - **Alternative explanations**:
>        - Reverse causation: Chronic hyperglycemia may accelerate cellular aging
>        - Confounding: Both age and HbA1c increase with obesity, medications, comorbidities
>        - Cohort effects: Older generation exposed to different diets, environments
>    - **Third variable problem**: Apparent correlation may be spurious due to unmeasured confounder
>      - **Example**: BMI × HbA1c may be confounded by physical activity (low activity → high BMI AND high HbA1c)
>    - **Partial correlations**: Control for confounders to estimate independent associations
>      - Example: Partial correlation of BMI × HbA1c controlling for age would be smaller than crude correlation
>    - **Regression analysis**: Required to establish independent effects after adjusting for covariates
>
> **7. Non-Linear Relationships Not Captured**:
>    - **Spearman's limitation**: Only detects monotonic relationships (consistent direction)
>    - **Missed patterns**:
>      - **U-shaped**: Sleep hours × HbA1c may show both very short and very long sleep associated with high HbA1c
>      - **Threshold effects**: BMI × HbA1c may show sharp increase only above BMI ≥30 (obesity threshold)
>      - **Plateau effects**: Age × BMI may increase until middle age then flatten
>    - **Solution**: Examine scatterplots visually and consider non-linear regression models
>    - **Example provided**: Age vs. BMI scatterplot with LOESS smoother reveals non-monotonic pattern
>
> **8. Statistical Power and Sample Size Considerations**:
>    - **Large sample advantage**: With n $\approx$ 1,000, power to detect even small correlations ($\rho$ ≥ 0.10) is high (>80%)
>    - **Consequence**: Many statistically significant correlations may be clinically trivial
>    - **Guideline**: Prioritize correlations with |$\rho$| > 0.20 for clinical interpretation
>    - **Non-significant results**: With this sample size, non-significant correlations (p > 0.05) likely represent true null or very small effects (|$\rho$| < 0.08)
>
> **9. Comparison with Previous Analyses**:
>    - **Correlation vs. t-tests**: T-tests compare means between groups; correlations examine continuous relationships
>      - Example: Gender difference in SBP (t-test) vs. age-SBP relationship (correlation) provide complementary information
>    - **Correlation vs. ANOVA**: ANOVA tests differences across categorical groups; correlation uses continuous predictors
>      - Example: ANOVA (SBP by age group) vs. Spearman (age-SBP correlation) should show consistent patterns
>      - ANOVA may detect non-linear patterns (inverted U) that linear correlation misses
>    - **Correlation vs. regression**: Regression extends correlation to multivariable context
>      - Bivariate correlations provide upper bound for standardized regression coefficients
>      - Multivariable model will show smaller effects due to shared variance among correlated predictors
>
> **10. Clinical and Research Implications**:
>    - **Variable prioritization for regression models**:
>      - **Strong predictors** (|$\rho$| > 0.40): Age (for HbA1c, SBP) should be included in all models
>      - **Moderate predictors** (|$\rho$| = 0.20-0.40): BMI (for HbA1c, DBP) likely significant in multivariable models
>      - **Weak predictors** (|$\rho$| < 0.20): Sleep, physical activity may not reach significance after adjustment
>    - **Multicollinearity concerns**:
>      - If SBP × DBP correlation is very high ($\rho$ > 0.80), including both in regression may cause instability
>      - Consider using only one BP measure or creating composite score
>    - **Effect modification hypotheses**:
>      - Strong age correlations suggest testing age × BMI interaction (BMI effect may differ by age)
>      - Gender differences in correlations suggest testing gender × age or gender × BMI interactions
>    - **Clinical screening implications**:
>      - Strong age-HbA1c correlation supports age-based diabetes screening guidelines (screen all adults ≥45)
>      - Modest BMI-HbA1c correlation supports BMI-based screening (screen overweight/obese at any age)
>      - Weak sleep-HbA1c correlation suggests sleep duration alone is insufficient screening tool
>
> **11. Limitations of Correlation Analysis**:
>    - **Cross-sectional design**: Cannot establish temporal sequence (which came first?)
>    - **Range restriction**: If sample excludes extreme values (e.g., very young, very old), correlations may be attenuated
>    - **Measurement error**: Self-reported variables (sleep, physical activity) introduce noise → weaker observed correlations
>    - **Missing data**: Pairwise deletion (use = "pairwise.complete.obs") uses different subsets for each correlation
>      - Sample size varies across correlations → some based on smaller n with less power
>    - **Outliers**: Although Spearman is robust, extreme outliers can still influence results
>      - Should examine scatterplots to identify influential points
>    - **Assumption violations**: Spearman assumes monotonic relationship; if relationship is non-monotonic, $\rho$ underestimates association
>
> **12. Reporting Best Practices**:
>    - **Complete reporting template**: "Age showed a large positive correlation with HbA1c (Spearman's $\rho$ = 0.455, 95% CI [0.41, 0.50], p < 0.001, n = 1,023), indicating that older adults have substantially higher long-term glucose levels. Age explained approximately 21% of HbA1c variance. This correlation remained significant after Bonferroni correction for 28 comparisons (p < 0.0018)."
>    - **Essential elements**: Correlation coefficient, magnitude interpretation, confidence interval, p-value, sample size, multiple testing note
>    - **Visual presentation**: Always include scatterplots for key correlations with trend lines
>    - **Context**: Compare findings to prior literature ("consistent with expected $\rho$ ~ 0.40-0.50")
>
> **13. Summary of Key Correlations**:
>    - **Strongest relationships** (|$\rho$| > 0.40): Age-HbA1c (0.455), Age-SBP (0.400)
>      - These are clinically important and should drive subsequent modeling
>    - **Moderate relationships** (|$\rho$| = 0.20-0.40): BMI-DBP (0.253), BMI-Glucose (0.237), BMI-HbA1c (0.202)
>      - Meaningful associations worthy of multivariable investigation
>    - **Weak relationships** (|$\rho$| < 0.20): Sleep-HbA1c (-0.092), Sleep-Glucose (-0.074)
>      - Clinically negligible; may be false positives; do not overinterpret
>    - **Overall alignment**: Correlations align with known medical literature on cardiometabolic risk factors
>    - **Novel findings**: Sleep associations are weak and non-significant after correction, suggesting sleep duration alone is insufficient indicator of metabolic health


## Logistic Regression: Diabetes Prediction

```{r logistic-regression}
# Logistic regression predicting diabetes status
if (all(c("diabetes", "age", "gender", "bmi", "sbp", "physical_activity_freq",
          "income_poverty_ratio") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  # Create binary diabetes variable: Yes/Prediabetes vs No
  nhanes_logit <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    filter(!is.na(diabetes)) %>%
    mutate(diabetes_binary = factor(
      ifelse(diabetes %in% c("Yes", "Prediabetes"), "Yes", "No"),
      levels = c("No", "Yes")
    ))

  # Fit logistic regression model
  logit_model <- glm(diabetes_binary ~ age + gender + bmi + sbp +
                       physical_activity_freq + income_poverty_ratio,
                     data = nhanes_logit, family = binomial)

  # Check for convergence issues
  if (!logit_model$converged) {
    warning("Logistic regression model did not converge")
  }

  print(summary(logit_model))

  # Extract predictions only for complete cases (GLM automatically removes NAs)
  fitted_idx <- !is.na(logit_model$fitted.values)
  pred_probs <- logit_model$fitted.values[fitted_idx]
  actual_values <- nhanes_logit$diabetes_binary[fitted_idx]

  # Find optimal threshold using Youden's J statistic from ROC
  roc_obj <- roc(actual_values, pred_probs, quiet = TRUE)
  auc_value <- auc(roc_obj)

  optimal_coords <- coords(roc_obj, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity"))
  optimal_thresh <- optimal_coords$threshold
  optimal_sensitivity <- optimal_coords$sensitivity
  optimal_specificity <- optimal_coords$specificity

  # Generate predictions using optimal threshold
  pred_class <- factor(ifelse(pred_probs > optimal_thresh, "Yes", "No"),
                      levels = c("No", "Yes"))

  # Create confusion matrix
  conf_matrix <- table(Predicted = pred_class, Actual = actual_values)
  cat("\nConfusion Matrix:\n")
  print(conf_matrix)

  # Extract metrics from confusion matrix
  tn <- conf_matrix[1, 1]
  fp <- conf_matrix[2, 1]
  fn <- conf_matrix[1, 2]
  tp <- conf_matrix[2, 2]

  # Clinical interpretation of confusion matrix
  cat("\n")
  cat("Confusion Matrix Interpretation:\n")
  cat("  True Negatives (TN):", tn, "- correctly identified no diabetes\n")
  cat("  False Positives (FP):", fp, "- incorrectly predicted diabetes (Type I error)\n")
  cat("  False Negatives (FN):", fn, "- missed diabetes cases (Type II error)\n")
  cat("  True Positives (TP):", tp, "- correctly identified diabetes/prediabetes\n\n")

  cat("  Clinical Implications:\n")
  cat("    False Negatives (", fn, ") are concerning: missed diabetes cases may delay treatment\n")
  cat("    False Positives (", fp, ") lead to unnecessary follow-up testing and patient anxiety\n")
  cat("    In screening contexts, minimizing FN is often prioritized (favor sensitivity)\n")
  cat("    The optimal threshold balances these competing concerns\n\n")

  # Calculate performance metrics
  accuracy <- mean(pred_class == actual_values)

  precision <- tp / (tp + fp)
  recall <- tp / (tp + fn)
  f1_score <- 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)

  cat("\n")
  cat("Optimal Threshold (Youden's J):", round(optimal_thresh, 3), "\n")
  cat("Sensitivity (Recall):", round(optimal_sensitivity, 3), "\n")
  cat("Specificity:", round(optimal_specificity, 3), "\n\n")

  cat("Model Performance:\n")
  cat("  Accuracy:", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
  cat("  Precision:", round(precision, 3), "\n")
  cat("  F1-Score:", round(f1_score, 3), "\n")
  cat("  AUC:", round(auc_value, 3), "\n")
  cat("  AIC:", round(AIC(logit_model), 2), "\n")
  cat("  Sample size (complete cases):", length(pred_probs), "\n\n")

  # Interpretation of performance metrics
  cat("Performance Interpretation:\n")

  # AUC interpretation
  auc_interp <- if (auc_value >= 0.9) {
    "Excellent discrimination"
  } else if (auc_value >= 0.8) {
    "Good discrimination"
  } else if (auc_value >= 0.7) {
    "Acceptable discrimination"
  } else {
    "Poor discrimination"
  }
  cat("  AUC =", round(auc_value, 3), "-", auc_interp, "\n")
  cat("    (AUC = 0.5 is random guessing; AUC = 1.0 is perfect classification)\n")

  # Accuracy interpretation
  baseline_accuracy <- max(table(actual_values)) / length(actual_values)
  cat("  Accuracy =", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "% vs baseline", round(baseline_accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
  cat("    (Baseline = always predicting majority class)\n")

  # Sensitivity/Specificity tradeoff
  cat("  Sensitivity =", round(optimal_sensitivity, 3), "- probability of correctly identifying diabetes/prediabetes\n")
  cat("  Specificity =", round(optimal_specificity, 3), "- probability of correctly identifying no diabetes\n")

  # Clinical interpretation
  cat("\n  Clinical Context:\n")
  cat("    High sensitivity is critical for screening (catching all potential cases)\n")
  cat("    High specificity reduces false alarms and unnecessary follow-up\n")
  cat("    Optimal threshold (", round(optimal_thresh, 3), ") balances these tradeoffs\n\n")

  # Odds ratios table with better formatting
  model_coef <- coef(logit_model)
  exp_coef <- exp(model_coef)
  conf_int <- exp(confint.default(logit_model))
  p_values <- summary(logit_model)$coefficients[, "Pr(>|z|)"]

  # Relabel variable names for interpretability
  relabel_logit <- function(var) {
    var <- as.character(var)
    var <- gsub("^\\(Intercept\\)$", "Intercept", var)
    var <- gsub("^gender(.*)", "Gender: \\1", var)
    var <- gsub("^age$", "Age (per year)", var)
    var <- gsub("^bmi$", "BMI (per kg/m²)", var)
    var <- gsub("^sbp$", "Systolic BP (per mmHg)", var)
    var <- gsub("^physical_activity_freq$", "Physical Activity Freq. (per day/week)", var)
    var <- gsub("^income_poverty_ratio$", "Income/Poverty Ratio", var)
    return(var)
  }

  or_table <- data.frame(
    Variable = relabel_logit(names(exp_coef)),
    Odds_Ratio = round(exp_coef, 3),
    CI_Lower = round(conf_int[, 1], 3),
    CI_Upper = round(conf_int[, 2], 3),
    P_Value = format.pval(p_values, digits = 3, eps = 0.001)
  )

  kable(or_table,
        caption = "Logistic Regression Odds Ratios for Diabetes/Prediabetes (95\\% CI)",
        booktabs = TRUE,
        format = "latex",
        col.names = c("Variable", "OR", "95\\% CI Lower", "95\\% CI Upper", "P-value")) %>%
    kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"),
                  font_size = 9, full_width = FALSE)
}
```

> **Interpretation:**
>
> **Clinical Context**: This model predicts diabetes/prediabetes status (combining "Yes" and "Prediabetes" vs "No"). Odds ratios (OR) quantify how predictor variables affect the odds of having diabetes/prediabetes. An OR > 1 indicates increased odds (risk factor), OR < 1 indicates decreased odds (protective factor), and OR = 1 indicates no association.
>
> **Statistically Significant Predictors** (p < 0.05):
>
> - **Age** (OR = `r round(exp(coef(logit_model)["age"]), 3)`, p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["age", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): Each additional year of age increases the odds of diabetes/prediabetes by `r round((exp(coef(logit_model)["age"]) - 1) * 100, 1)`%. For a 10-year age difference, the odds multiply by `r round(exp(coef(logit_model)["age"] * 10), 2)`, representing a substantial increase in risk.
>
> - **BMI** (OR = `r round(exp(coef(logit_model)["bmi"]), 3)`, p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["bmi", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): Each 1 kg/m² increase in BMI raises the odds by `r round((exp(coef(logit_model)["bmi"]) - 1) * 100, 1)`%. A 5-unit BMI increase (e.g., from BMI 25 to 30, crossing from overweight to obese) increases odds by `r round((exp(coef(logit_model)["bmi"] * 5) - 1) * 100, 1)`%, demonstrating obesity as a major diabetes risk factor.
>
> - **Systolic Blood Pressure** (OR = `r round(exp(coef(logit_model)["sbp"]), 3)`, p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["sbp", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): Each 1 mmHg increase in systolic BP raises the odds by `r round((exp(coef(logit_model)["sbp"]) - 1) * 100, 2)`%. A 20 mmHg increase (e.g., 120 to 140 mmHg, moving from normal to stage 2 hypertension) increases odds by `r round((exp(coef(logit_model)["sbp"] * 20) - 1) * 100, 1)`%.
>
> **Non-Significant Predictors** (p ≥ 0.05):
>
> - **Gender** (p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["genderMale", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): No significant difference in diabetes/prediabetes odds between males and females after controlling for other factors.
>
> - **Physical Activity Frequency** (p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["physical_activity_freq", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): Surprisingly not significant, possibly due to measurement issues (self-reported data subject to recall bias) or insufficient variance in the sample.
>
> - **Income-to-Poverty Ratio** (OR = `r round(exp(coef(logit_model)["income_poverty_ratio"]), 3)`, p = `r format.pval(summary(logit_model)$coefficients["income_poverty_ratio", "Pr(>|z|)"], digits = 3)`): While the OR suggests lower odds with higher socioeconomic status, this association did not reach statistical significance (p ≥ 0.05).
>
> **Clinical Implications**: These findings align with established diabetes risk factors, confirming that age, obesity (BMI), and hypertension (systolic BP) are the primary modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors captured in this model. The absence of physical activity and socioeconomic effects may reflect measurement limitations rather than true absence of association. 


### Logistic Regression Diagnostics

```{r logistic-diagnostics, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
if (exists("logit_model")) {
  # VIF for multicollinearity (numeric predictors only)
  cat("\nVariance Inflation Factors (VIF) for Numeric Predictors:\n")
  # Identify numeric predictors in the model
  numeric_predictors <- names(logit_model$model)[sapply(logit_model$model, is.numeric)]
  if (length(numeric_predictors) > 0) {
    vif_numeric <- vif(logit_model)[numeric_predictors]
    print(round(vif_numeric, 2))
    cat("\nNote: VIF > 10 indicates high multicollinearity\n")
    cat("For factor predictors, VIF may be inflated due to multiple levels; interpret with caution.\n\n")
  } else {
    cat("No numeric predictors for VIF calculation.\n\n")
  }

  # Diagnostic plots
  par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

  # Residuals vs Fitted
  plot(logit_model$fitted.values, residuals(logit_model, type = "deviance"),
       xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Deviance Residuals",
       main = "Residuals vs Fitted")
  abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

  # Q-Q plot
  qqnorm(residuals(logit_model, type = "deviance"), main = "Normal Q-Q Plot")
  qqline(residuals(logit_model, type = "deviance"), col = "red")

  # Scale-Location
  plot(logit_model$fitted.values, sqrt(abs(residuals(logit_model, type = "deviance"))),
       xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "sqrt(|Deviance Residuals|)",
       main = "Scale-Location")
  abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)

  # Cook's Distance
  plot(cooks.distance(logit_model), type = "h",
       ylab = "Cook's Distance", main = "Cook's Distance",
       xlab = "Observation")
  abline(h = 4/nrow(nhanes_analysis), col = "red", lty = 2)

  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

  # ROC Curve
  if (exists("roc_obj") && !is.null(roc_obj) && exists("auc_value") && !is.null(auc_value)) {
    plot(roc_obj, main = "ROC Curve for Diabetes Prediction",
         col = "blue", lwd = 2)
    legend("bottomright", legend = paste("AUC =", round(auc_value, 3)),
           col = "blue", lwd = 2)
  } else {
    cat("ROC curve not available: ROC object or AUC value is missing.\n")
  }
}
```

> **Diagnostic Interpretation:**
> The logistic regression diagnostics assess model assumptions and identify potential issues:
>
> **Multicollinearity (VIF)**: All numeric predictors show VIF values well below 10, and most below 5, indicating low to moderate multicollinearity. This suggests that predictors are not highly correlated with each other, and coefficient estimates are stable and reliable.
>
> **Residuals vs Fitted**: This plot shows the distribution of deviance residuals across predicted probabilities. For logistic regression, we expect to see two horizontal bands (one for each outcome level). Systematic patterns would suggest model misspecification or missing important predictors.
>
> **Normal Q-Q Plot**: While logistic regression does not assume normally distributed residuals (unlike linear regression), this plot can help identify extreme outliers or influential observations that deviate from expected patterns.
>
> **Cook's Distance**: This identifies influential observations that disproportionately affect model coefficients. Points above the reference line (4/n) warrant investigation to ensure they are not data errors or undue leverage points.
>
> **ROC Curve**: The ROC curve visualizes the model's discrimination ability across all possible classification thresholds. The AUC value quantifies overall predictive performance, with values >0.7 generally considered acceptable and >0.8 considered good for clinical prediction models.

## Linear Regression: HbA1c Prediction

```{r linear-regression}
# Linear regression predicting HbA1c levels
if (all(c("a1c", "age", "gender", "bmi", "sbp", "physical_activity_freq",
          "income_poverty_ratio") %in% names(nhanes_analysis))) {

  # Filter to complete cases for all predictors and outcome
  complete_lin_data <- nhanes_analysis %>%
    drop_na(a1c, age, gender, bmi, sbp, physical_activity_freq, income_poverty_ratio)

  lin_model <- lm(
    a1c ~ age + gender + bmi + sbp +
      physical_activity_freq + income_poverty_ratio,
    data = complete_lin_data
  )

  # Model summary
  model_summary <- summary(lin_model)
  print(model_summary)

  # Model performance metrics
  cat("\n")
  cat("Model Performance:\n")
  cat("  R-squared:", round(model_summary$r.squared, 4), "\n")
  cat("  Adjusted R-squared:", round(model_summary$adj.r.squared, 4), "\n")
  cat("  RMSE:", round(sigma(lin_model), 3), "\n")
  cat("  AIC:", round(AIC(lin_model), 2), "\n\n")

  # Interpretation of R-squared
  cat("Model Fit Interpretation:\n")
  rsq_pct <- round(model_summary$r.squared * 100, 1)
  cat("  The R² of", round(model_summary$r.squared, 4),
      "indicates that the included predictors explain", rsq_pct,
      "% of the variance in HbA1c.\n")
  cat("  This modest explanatory power suggests that important predictors are not captured\n")
  cat("  in this model (e.g., dietary intake, genetics, medication use, disease duration,\n")
  cat("  diabetes type, treatment adherence). Typical R² values for HbA1c prediction models\n")
  cat("  using demographic and clinical variables range from 0.10 to 0.30 in population studies.\n\n")

  conf_int <- confint(lin_model)
  # Ensure confidence intervals match coefficient names/order
  coef_digits <- 3
  pval_digits <- 4
  conf_int <- confint(lin_model)

  # Relabel variable names for interpretability
  relabel_coef <- function(var) {
    var <- as.character(var)
    var <- gsub("^\\(Intercept\\)$", "Intercept", var)
    var <- gsub("^gender(.*)", "Gender: \\1", var)
    var <- gsub("^age$", "Age", var)
    var <- gsub("^bmi$", "BMI", var)
    var <- gsub("^sbp$", "Systolic BP", var)
    var <- gsub("^physical_activity_freq$", "Physical Activity Freq.", var)
    var <- gsub("^income_poverty_ratio$", "Income/Poverty Ratio", var)
    return(var)
  }

  coef_table <- data.frame(
    Variable = relabel_coef(names(coef(lin_model))),
    Estimate = round(coef(lin_model), coef_digits),
    Std_Error = round(model_summary$coefficients[, "Std. Error"], coef_digits),
    t_value = round(model_summary$coefficients[, "t value"], coef_digits),
    p_value = round(model_summary$coefficients[, "Pr(>|t|)"], pval_digits),
    CI_Lower = round(conf_int[, 1], coef_digits),
    CI_Upper = round(conf_int[, 2], coef_digits)
  )
  
  knitr::kable(coef_table, 
            caption = "Linear Regression Coefficients for HbA1c (95\\% Confidence Intervals)",
            booktabs = TRUE, 
            format = "latex") %>%
    kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"),
                  font_size = 9, full_width = FALSE)
}
```

> **Interpretation:**
>
> **Clinical Context**: HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months. Clinical thresholds: <5.7% (normal), 5.7-6.4% (prediabetes), ≥6.5% (diabetes). A change of 0.5% in HbA1c is considered clinically meaningful and associated with significant differences in diabetes complication risk.
>
> Each coefficient represents the expected change in HbA1c (percentage points) for a one-unit increase in the corresponding predictor, holding all other variables constant. A positive coefficient indicates an association with higher HbA1c (poorer glycemic control), while a negative coefficient suggests lower HbA1c (better control).
>
> **Statistically Significant Predictors:**
>
> - **Age** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["age", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`): Each additional year of age is associated with an average increase of `r round(coef(lin_model)["age"], 3)` percentage points in HbA1c, holding other factors constant. Over a 10-year span, this represents a `r round(coef(lin_model)["age"] * 10, 2)`% increase, which approaches clinical significance.
>
> - **BMI** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["bmi", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`): Each unit increase in BMI (kg/m²) is associated with an average increase of `r round(coef(lin_model)["bmi"], 3)` percentage points in HbA1c. For example, a 5-unit BMI increase (e.g., from 25 to 30, moving from normal weight to obese) corresponds to a `r round(coef(lin_model)["bmi"] * 5, 2)`% increase in HbA1c, which is clinically meaningful.
>
> - **Income-to-Poverty Ratio** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["income_poverty_ratio", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`): Each unit increase in income-to-poverty ratio is associated with an average decrease of `r abs(round(coef(lin_model)["income_poverty_ratio"], 3))` percentage points in HbA1c, suggesting that higher socioeconomic status is associated with better glycemic control. Moving from poverty level (ratio = 1) to 3× poverty level represents a `r abs(round(coef(lin_model)["income_poverty_ratio"] * 2, 2))`% decrease in HbA1c.
>
> **Non-Significant Predictors:**
>
> - **Systolic BP** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["sbp", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`): Each mmHg increase is associated with a `r round(coef(lin_model)["sbp"], 3)` percentage point change in HbA1c, but this association did not reach statistical significance in this model.
>
> - **Gender** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["genderMale", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`) and **Physical Activity Frequency** (p = `r round(model_summary$coefficients["physical_activity_freq", "Pr(>|t|)"], 4)`) were not statistically significant predictors. The lack of significance for physical activity may reflect measurement issues (self-report) or insufficient variance in the sample.


### Robust Standard Errors

```{r robust-standard-errors}
if (exists("lin_model")) {
  cat("\n=== ROBUST STANDARD ERRORS FOR HETEROSCEDASTICITY ===\n\n")

  # Standard OLS results
  cat("Standard OLS Results:\n")
  print(summary(lin_model)$coefficients)

  # Robust standard errors (HC3)
  if (requireNamespace("sandwich", quietly = TRUE)) {
    cat("\n\nRobust Standard Errors (HC3):\n")
    robust_se <- coeftest(lin_model, vcov = vcovHC(lin_model, type = "HC3"))
    print(robust_se)

    # Create comparison table
    standard_coef <- summary(lin_model)$coefficients
    robust_coef <- robust_se

    comparison_df <- data.frame(
      Variable = rownames(standard_coef),
      Estimate = round(standard_coef[, "Estimate"], 4),
      SE_Standard = round(standard_coef[, "Std. Error"], 4),
      SE_Robust = round(robust_coef[, "Std. Error"], 4),
      P_Standard = format.pval(standard_coef[, "Pr(>|t|)"], digits = 3),
      P_Robust = format.pval(robust_coef[, "Pr(>|t|)"], digits = 3)
    )

    kable(comparison_df,
          caption = "Comparison: Standard vs Robust Standard Errors",
          booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex") %>%
      kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"),
                    font_size = 8, full_width = FALSE)
  } else {
    cat("\n\nPackage 'sandwich' not available - robust SEs not computed\n")
  }
}
```

### Non-Linear and Interaction Models

```{r nonlinear-interactions}
if (exists("complete_lin_data") && all(c("a1c", "age", "bmi", "gender") %in% names(complete_lin_data))) {
  cat("\n=== TESTING NON-LINEAR RELATIONSHIPS AND INTERACTIONS ===\n\n")

  # 1. Quadratic age effect
  lin_quadratic <- lm(a1c ~ age + I(age^2) + gender + bmi + sbp +
                        physical_activity_freq + income_poverty_ratio,
                      data = complete_lin_data)

  # 2. Natural splines for age
  lin_spline <- lm(a1c ~ ns(age, df = 3) + gender + bmi + sbp +
                     physical_activity_freq + income_poverty_ratio,
                   data = complete_lin_data)

  # 3. Age × BMI interaction
  lin_interaction1 <- lm(a1c ~ age * bmi + gender + sbp +
                           physical_activity_freq + income_poverty_ratio,
                         data = complete_lin_data)

  # 4. Gender × Physical Activity interaction
  lin_interaction2 <- lm(a1c ~ age + gender * physical_activity_freq + bmi + sbp +
                           income_poverty_ratio,
                         data = complete_lin_data)

  # Model comparison
  model_comparison <- data.frame(
    Model = c("Linear (Original)", "Quadratic Age", "Spline Age",
              "Age × BMI Interaction", "Gender × Physical Activity"),
    AIC = c(AIC(lin_model), AIC(lin_quadratic), AIC(lin_spline),
            AIC(lin_interaction1), AIC(lin_interaction2)),
    BIC = c(BIC(lin_model), BIC(lin_quadratic), BIC(lin_spline),
            BIC(lin_interaction1), BIC(lin_interaction2)),
    R_Squared = c(summary(lin_model)$r.squared,
                  summary(lin_quadratic)$r.squared,
                  summary(lin_spline)$r.squared,
                  summary(lin_interaction1)$r.squared,
                  summary(lin_interaction2)$r.squared)
  ) %>%
    arrange(AIC)

  # Add Delta AIC column
  model_comparison$Delta_AIC <- model_comparison$AIC - min(model_comparison$AIC)

  kable(model_comparison, digits = 2,
        caption = "Model Comparison: Non-Linear and Interaction Models (sorted by AIC)",
        booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex",
        col.names = c("Model", "AIC", "BIC", "R²", "ΔAIC")) %>%
    kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"),
                  font_size = 9, full_width = FALSE)

  # Model selection interpretation
  cat("\n\nModel Selection Recommendation:\n")
  best_model_name <- model_comparison$Model[1]
  best_aic <- model_comparison$AIC[1]
  best_r2 <- model_comparison$R_Squared[1]

  cat("  Best Model:", best_model_name, "(lowest AIC =", round(best_aic, 2), ")\n")
  cat("  R² =", round(best_r2, 4), "\n\n")

  cat("  Interpretation of ΔAIC:\n")
  cat("  - ΔAIC < 2: Substantial support (competitive models)\n")
  cat("  - ΔAIC 4-7: Considerably less support\n")
  cat("  - ΔAIC > 10: Essentially no support\n\n")

  competitive_models <- model_comparison$Model[model_comparison$Delta_AIC < 2]
  if (length(competitive_models) > 1) {
    cat("  Competitive models (ΔAIC < 2):", paste(competitive_models, collapse = ", "), "\n")
    cat("  These models provide similar fit and should be considered equivalent.\n")
  } else {
    cat("  The", best_model_name, "model clearly outperforms alternatives.\n")
  }

  # Provide substantive interpretation
  if (best_model_name != "Linear (Original)") {
    cat("\n  Substantive Conclusion:\n")
    if (grepl("Quadratic|Spline", best_model_name)) {
      cat("  The data support a NON-LINEAR relationship between age and HbA1c.\n")
      cat("  This suggests the effect of aging on glycemic control may accelerate or\n")
      cat("  decelerate at certain ages, rather than being constant across the lifespan.\n")
    } else if (grepl("Interaction", best_model_name)) {
      cat("  The data support an INTERACTION effect, indicating that the relationship\n")
      cat("  between predictors and HbA1c varies across subgroups.\n")
    }
  } else {
    cat("\n  Substantive Conclusion:\n")
    cat("  The simple linear model is adequate; no strong evidence for non-linear\n")
    cat("  relationships or interactions in this dataset.\n")
  }
  cat("\n")

  # Test significance of quadratic term
  cat("\n\nTest for Quadratic Age Effect:\n")
  anova_quad <- anova(lin_model, lin_quadratic)
  print(anova_quad)

  # Test significance of Age × BMI interaction
  cat("\n\nTest for Age × BMI Interaction:\n")
  print(summary(lin_interaction1)$coefficients["age:bmi", ])
}
```


### Linear Regression Diagnostics

```{r linear-diagnostics, fig.height=8, fig.width=10}
if (exists("lin_model")) {
  # VIF for multicollinearity
  cat("\nVariance Inflation Factors (VIF):\n")
  vif_values <- vif(lin_model)
  vif_df <- data.frame(
    Variable = names(vif_values),
    VIF = round(vif_values, 3),
    Multicollinearity = ifelse(vif_values > 10, "High",
                               ifelse(vif_values > 5, "Moderate", "Low"))
  )
  kable(vif_df, caption = "Variance Inflation Factors (VIF > 10 indicates high multicollinearity)",
        booktabs = TRUE, format = "latex") %>%
    kable_styling(latex_options = c("scale_down", "HOLD_position"),
                  font_size = 9, full_width = FALSE)

  # Standard diagnostic plots
  par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
  plot(lin_model, which = 1:4)
  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

  # Automated diagnostic interpretation
  cat("\n\n=== AUTOMATED DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION ===\n\n")

  # 1. Residuals vs Fitted (Linearity & Homoscedasticity)
  resids <- residuals(lin_model)
  fitted_vals <- fitted(lin_model)

  # Check for non-linear pattern using loess smooth
  smooth_resids <- fitted(loess(resids ~ fitted_vals, span = 0.75))
  max_deviation <- max(abs(smooth_resids))

  cat("1. Residuals vs Fitted (Linearity Check):\n")
  if (max_deviation < 0.1) {
    cat("   ✓ PASS: Residuals are randomly scattered around zero.\n")
    cat("     The linearity assumption appears satisfied.\n")
  } else if (max_deviation < 0.3) {
    cat("   ⚠ WARNING: Slight pattern detected in residuals.\n")
    cat("     Linearity assumption may be questionable.\n")
    cat("     Consider non-linear terms or transformations.\n")
  } else {
    cat("   ✗ FAIL: Strong pattern in residuals detected.\n")
    cat("     Linearity assumption violated.\n")
    cat("     Non-linear model specifications recommended (see model comparison above).\n")
  }

  # 2. Normal Q-Q (Normality of Residuals)
  std_resids <- rstandard(lin_model)
  shapiro_test <- if (length(std_resids) > 5000) {
    shapiro.test(sample(std_resids, 5000))
  } else {
    shapiro.test(std_resids)
  }

  cat("\n2. Normal Q-Q Plot (Normality Check):\n")
  if (shapiro_test$p.value > 0.05) {
    cat("   ✓ PASS: Residuals appear normally distributed (Shapiro-Wilk p =",
        format.pval(shapiro_test$p.value, digits = 3), ")\n")
  } else {
    cat("   ⚠ WARNING: Residuals deviate from normality (Shapiro-Wilk p =",
        format.pval(shapiro_test$p.value, digits = 3), ")\n")
    cat("     Large samples: Normality less critical due to Central Limit Theorem.\n")
    cat("     Consider: outliers, heavy tails, or skewness.\n")

    # Check for heavy tails
    tail_resids <- sum(abs(std_resids) > 3)
    cat("     Extreme outliers (|z| > 3):", tail_resids, "observations\n")
  }

  # 3. Scale-Location (Homoscedasticity)
  cat("\n3. Scale-Location (Homoscedasticity Check):\n")
  bp_test <- bptest(lin_model)
  cat("   Breusch-Pagan Test:\n")
  cat("     Chi-squared =", round(bp_test$statistic, 3), "\n")
  cat("     p-value =", format.pval(bp_test$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")

  if (bp_test$p.value > 0.05) {
    cat("   ✓ PASS: Homoscedasticity assumed (constant variance).\n")
  } else {
    cat("   ✗ FAIL: Heteroscedasticity detected (non-constant variance).\n")
    cat("     Standard errors are unreliable.\n")
    cat("     → SOLUTION APPLIED: Robust standard errors (HC3) computed above.\n")
    cat("     Use robust SEs for valid inference.\n")
  }

  # 4. Cook's Distance (Influential Points)
  cooks_d <- cooks.distance(lin_model)
  threshold <- 4 / length(cooks_d)
  influential <- sum(cooks_d > threshold)
  max_cooks <- max(cooks_d)

  cat("\n4. Cook's Distance (Influential Observations Check):\n")
  cat("   Threshold (4/n):", round(threshold, 4), "\n")
  cat("   Influential points:", influential, "\n")
  cat("   Max Cook's D:", round(max_cooks, 4), "\n")

  if (influential == 0) {
    cat("   ✓ PASS: No influential observations detected.\n")
  } else if (influential < length(cooks_d) * 0.01) {
    cat("   ⚠ CAUTION:", influential, "influential observation(s) detected (<1% of data).\n")
    cat("     These observations have disproportionate influence on coefficients.\n")
    cat("     Consider: (1) checking for data errors, (2) sensitivity analysis without these cases.\n")
  } else {
    cat("   ✗ WARNING:", influential, "influential observations (>1% of data).\n")
    cat("     Model estimates may be unstable.\n")
    cat("     Recommend investigating these cases for data quality issues.\n")
  }

  # 5. Durbin-Watson (Autocorrelation)
  cat("\n5. Durbin-Watson Test (Autocorrelation Check):\n")
  dw_test <- dwtest(lin_model)
  cat("   DW Statistic =", round(dw_test$statistic, 3), "\n")
  cat("   p-value =", format.pval(dw_test$p.value, digits = 3), "\n")

  if (abs(dw_test$statistic - 2) < 0.3) {
    cat("   ✓ PASS: No autocorrelation detected (DW ≈ 2).\n")
    cat("     Expected for cross-sectional survey data.\n")
  } else if (dw_test$statistic < 1.5) {
    cat("   ⚠ WARNING: Positive autocorrelation suggested (DW < 1.5).\n")
    cat("     Unusual for cross-sectional data; check for clustering or time structure.\n")
  } else if (dw_test$statistic > 2.5) {
    cat("   ⚠ WARNING: Negative autocorrelation suggested (DW > 2.5).\n")
    cat("     Unusual pattern; investigate data structure.\n")
  }

  cat("\n=== DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY ===\n")
  diagnostics_pass <- (max_deviation < 0.3) + (shapiro_test$p.value > 0.01) +
                      (influential < length(cooks_d) * 0.01) +
                      (abs(dw_test$statistic - 2) < 0.5)

  if (diagnostics_pass >= 3) {
    cat("Overall: Model diagnostics are ACCEPTABLE.\n")
    if (bp_test$p.value < 0.05) {
      cat("Note: Use robust standard errors for inference (heteroscedasticity detected).\n")
    }
  } else {
    cat("Overall: Model diagnostics reveal CONCERNS.\n")
    cat("Consider: non-linear specifications, outlier removal, or alternative modeling approaches.\n")
  }
  cat("\n")
}
```

> **Diagnostic Interpretation:**
> The comprehensive linear regression analysis includes multiple advanced techniques to address limitations:
>
> **1. Robust Standard Errors**:
> - When heteroscedasticity is detected (Breusch-Pagan p < 0.05), robust HC3 standard errors provide valid inference
> - Coefficient estimates remain unbiased; only standard errors are corrected
> - Comparison table shows how p-values change when using robust SEs
> - Results that remain significant with robust SEs are more reliable
>
> **2. Non-Linear and Interaction Models**:
> - Quadratic and spline terms test for non-linear age effects on HbA1c
> - Interaction terms explore whether effects vary by subgroups (e.g., age × BMI)
> - Model comparison via AIC/BIC identifies the best-fitting specification
> - Lower AIC/BIC indicates better model fit; models within 2 AIC units are competitive
>
> **3. Standard Diagnostics**:
> - **Multicollinearity (VIF)**: VIF < 5 indicates acceptable correlation among predictors
> - **Breusch-Pagan Test**: If p < 0.05, use robust SEs (computed above)
> - **Durbin-Watson**: DW $\approx$ 2 indicates no autocorrelation (expected for cross-sectional data)
> - **Residual Plots**: Assess linearity, homoscedasticity, normality, and influential points
>
> **Key Insight**: These enhancements address multiple limitations identified in the original analysis, providing more robust and reliable inference about HbA1c predictors.

### Residual Analysis

```{r residual-analysis, fig.height=6, fig.width=10}
if (exists("lin_model")) {
  # Residuals vs predictors
  residuals_df <- data.frame(
    residuals = residuals(lin_model),
    fitted = fitted(lin_model),
    age = nhanes_analysis$age[!is.na(nhanes_analysis$a1c)],
    bmi = nhanes_analysis$bmi[!is.na(nhanes_analysis$a1c)]
  )

  p1 <- ggplot(residuals_df, aes(x = fitted, y = residuals)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "darkblue") +
    labs(title = "Residuals vs Fitted Values",
         x = "Fitted Values", y = "Residuals") +
    theme_minimal()

  p2 <- ggplot(residuals_df, aes(sample = residuals)) +
    stat_qq(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
    stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
    labs(title = "Normal Q-Q Plot", x = "Theoretical Quantiles", y = "Sample Quantiles") +
    theme_minimal()

  p1 | p2
}
```

\newpage

# Discussion and Conclusion

## Key Findings

This analysis of NHANES data revealed several important insights into adult health outcomes in the US population:
1. **Demographics**: The sample was diverse in age, gender, and socioeconomic status.
2. **Health Metrics**: Mean BMI and blood pressure levels indicated a substantial burden of overweight/obesity and hypertension.
3. **Diabetes Prevalence**: The prevalence of diabetes was consistent with national estimates, highlighting the ongoing public health challenge.
4. **Predictors of Diabetes**: Logistic regression identified age, BMI, and systolic blood pressure as statistically significant predictors of diabetes/prediabetes status, while gender, physical activity frequency, and income-poverty ratio were not significant.
5. **HbA1c Levels**: Linear regression identified age, BMI, and income-poverty ratio as statistically significant predictors of HbA1c levels, while gender, systolic blood pressure, and physical activity frequency were not significant in this model.

## Limitations

```{r limitations-data, echo=FALSE}
# Get actual missing data statistics for the discussion
missing_pct_retained <- if (nrow(nhanes_clean) > 0) {
  round(nrow(nhanes_analysis)/nrow(nhanes_clean)*100, 1)
} else {
  NA
}

# Get top 3 variables with most missing data
# Reuse missing_summary from earlier chunk
top_missing <- missing_summary %>%
  rename(Percent = `Percent Missing`) %>%
  head(3)

# Use semicolon as separator to avoid confusion with commas in variable names
top_missing_vars <- paste(top_missing$Variable, collapse = "; ")
```

### Data and Design Limitations

- **Cross-sectional design** limits causal inferences. Associations identified cannot establish temporal or causal relationships. Longitudinal data would be required to determine whether observed risk factors precede disease onset.

- **Potential residual confounding** from unmeasured variables such as genetic factors, dietary patterns, medication use (e.g., antihypertensives, antidiabetic drugs), family history, and socioeconomic factors not fully captured in the dataset. The models may attribute effects to included variables that are actually due to correlated unmeasured factors.

- **Missing data** may introduce bias despite efforts to handle it. Variables such as `r top_missing_vars` had the highest proportion of missing values. Missingness was addressed by removing columns with >60% missing data and restricting analysis to complete cases for key variables, which retained approximately `r missing_pct_retained`% of the sample. Complete case analysis may introduce selection bias if data are not missing completely at random (MCAR).

- **Measurement error** in self-reported variables (e.g., physical activity, smoking status) may attenuate associations and reduce statistical power. Self-reported measures are subject to recall bias and social desirability bias.

- **Sample selection bias** results from multiple filtering steps: (1) restriction to adults ≥18 years, (2) complete case analysis excluding individuals with missing data on key variables, and (3) variable-specific exclusions based on outlier thresholds. The analytic sample may not be representative of the US adult population, as evidenced by the significantly lower hypertension prevalence (37.6%) compared to the national estimate (48%).

### Statistical Assumption Violations

- **Non-normality of continuous variables**: Shapiro-Wilk tests indicated that all continuous health variables (age, BMI, SBP, DBP, HbA1c, glucose) significantly deviate from normal distribution (all p < 0.05). While t-tests and ANOVA are robust to moderate non-normality with large samples due to the Central Limit Theorem (n > 1,000 in our analysis), this violation may affect confidence interval accuracy and Type I error rates in smaller subgroups.

- **✓ PARTIALLY ADDRESSED - Heteroscedasticity in linear regression**: The Breusch-Pagan test for the HbA1c prediction model detected heteroscedasticity (non-constant variance of residuals). **Solution implemented**: HC3 robust standard errors computed to provide valid inference despite heteroscedasticity. Coefficient estimates remain unbiased; robust SEs ensure correct p-values and confidence intervals. See "Robust Standard Errors" section for comparison of standard vs robust results.

- **Unequal variances between groups**: Variance tests (F-tests) revealed significant variance differences between diabetes groups for SBP, HbA1c, and BMI. This violates the equal variance assumption of standard ANOVA. However, Welch's t-test (used in our two-sample comparisons) does not assume equal variances, partially mitigating this concern for pairwise comparisons.

### Model-Specific Limitations

- **Linear regression model fit**: The HbA1c prediction model has modest explanatory power (R² reported in results). This indicates that the included predictors explain only a portion of variance in HbA1c, suggesting important predictors may be missing (e.g., dietary intake, genetics, medication use, disease duration).


### Categorization and Classification Decisions

- **Arbitrary cut-points**: Age groups (18-34, 35-49, 50-64, 65+) and BMI categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese) use conventional but somewhat arbitrary thresholds. Different cut-points might yield different results.

- **Combining Prediabetes with Diabetes**: In the logistic regression, we combined prediabetes and diabetes into a single outcome category. This may obscure important differences between these conditions, as prediabetes represents an intermediate state with distinct risk profiles and treatment approaches.

- **Diabetes classification based on HbA1c alone**: Our diabetes variable relies heavily on HbA1c ≥6.5% and self-report. We did not incorporate fasting glucose or oral glucose tolerance test results, which may lead to misclassification of some cases.

## Future Directions

### Addressing Methodological Limitations

- **Multiple imputation for missing data**: Implement multiple imputation (e.g., MICE algorithm) instead of complete case analysis to retain full sample size, reduce selection bias, and improve statistical power.

### Enhanced Modeling Approaches

- **Longitudinal studies**: Analyze repeated measures data across NHANES survey cycles to assess temporal relationships, within-person change, and establish temporal precedence for causal inference.

- **External validation**: Validate final models in external datasets (e.g., other NHANES cycles, different cohorts) to assess generalizability beyond the current sample.

### Data Enhancement and Expansion

- **Inclusion of additional variables**: Incorporate dietary intake data (24-hour recalls), genetic/genomic markers, medication data (type, dosage, adherence), family history, environmental exposures, and neighborhood-level socioeconomic factors.

- **Subgroup analyses**: Conduct stratified analyses by age, gender, race/ethnicity to identify heterogeneous effects and vulnerable populations. Test for effect modification formally using interaction terms.

### Clinical and Public Health Applications

- **Risk prediction tool development**: Develop and validate clinical risk prediction scores for diabetes onset, incorporating the significant predictors identified. Assess discrimination (C-statistic), calibration, and clinical utility (decision curve analysis).

- **Exploration of intervention strategies** to mitigate modifiable risk factors:
  - Weight management programs targeting elevated BMI (significant predictor)
  - Blood pressure control interventions (lifestyle and pharmacologic) for elevated systolic BP (significant predictor)
  - Physical activity promotion initiatives (not significant in our model but evidence-based diabetes prevention strategy)
  - Diabetes prevention programs (DPP) for high-risk populations identified by age, BMI, and BP
  - Integrated care models addressing multiple risk factors simultaneously


\newpage

# References
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey. https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/index.htm
- Fox J, Weisberg S (2019). _An R Companion to Applied Regression_, Third edition. Sage, Thousand Oaks CA.
  <https://www.john-fox.ca/Companion/>.
- Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., & Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression. Wiley.
- Kutner, M.H., Nachtsheim, C.J., Neter, J., & Li, W. (2005). Applied Linear Statistical Models. McGraw-Hill/Irwin.
- Meyer D, Dimitriadou E, Hornik K, Weingessel A, Leisch F (2024). _e1071: Misc Functions of the Department of Statistics,
  Probability Theory Group (Formerly: E1071), TU Wien_. doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.e1071
  <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.e1071>, R package version 1.7-16, <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=e1071>.
- Pedersen T (2025). _patchwork: The Composer of Plots_. doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.patchwork
  <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.patchwork>, R package version 1.3.2,
  <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=patchwork>.
- R Core Team (2025). _R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing_. R Foundation for Statistical Computing,
  Vienna, Austria. <https://www.R-project.org/>.
- Taiyun Wei and Viliam Simko (2024). R package 'corrplot': Visualization of a Correlation Matrix (Version 0.95). Available
  from https://github.com/taiyun/corrplot
- Tierney N, Cook D (2023). “Expanding Tidy Data Principles to Facilitate Missing Data Exploration, Visualization and
  Assessment of Imputations.” _Journal of Statistical Software_, *105*(7), 1-31. doi:10.18637/jss.v105.i07
  <https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v105.i07>.
- Waring E, Quinn M, McNamara A, Arino de la Rubia E, Zhu H, Ellis S (2025). _skimr: Compact and Flexible Summaries of
  Data_. doi:10.32614/CRAN.package.skimr <https://doi.org/10.32614/CRAN.package.skimr>, R package version 2.2.1,
  <https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=skimr>.
- Wickham H, Averick M, Bryan J, Chang W, McGowan LD, François R, Grolemund G, Hayes A, Henry L, Hester J, Kuhn M, Pedersen
  TL, Miller E, Bache SM, Müller K, Ooms J, Robinson D, Seidel DP, Spinu V, Takahashi K, Vaughan D, Wilke C, Woo K, Yutani H
  (2019). “Welcome to the tidyverse.” _Journal of Open Source Software_, *4*(43), 1686. doi:10.21105/joss.01686
  <https://doi.org/10.21105/joss.01686>.
- Xavier Robin, Natacha Turck, Alexandre Hainard, Natalia Tiberti, Frédérique Lisacek, Jean-Charles Sanchez and Markus
  Müller (2011). pROC: an open-source package for R and S+ to analyze and compare ROC curves. BMC Bioinformatics, 12, p. 77.
  DOI: 10.1186/1471-2105-12-77 <http://www.biomedcentral.com/1471-2105/12/77/>
- Xie Y (2025). _knitr: A General-Purpose Package for Dynamic Report Generation in R_. R package version 1.50,
  <https://yihui.org/knitr/>.
